input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 industrial serial robot with six axes, operating in a manufacturing environment. Key components include the base axis, joints, and mechanical structure. It is suitable for digital twinning due to its complex dynamic behavior, requirement for precise multi-physics-aware friction modeling, and real-time monitoring needs to enhance operational accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including continuous multi-physics-aware joint friction identification, accuracy improvement through real-time adjustments based on sensor data, and dashboard-based visualization of system state and performance metrics. These services target manufacturing engineers and maintenance personnel for monitoring, optimization, and troubleshooting of robotic operations in industrial settings.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: MBSE employs SysML for modeling across requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical abstraction levels, utilizing methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define components, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavior is modeled using MontiArc Automaton, SysML v1.7 state machines, MathWorks Simulink, and general-purpose languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system maintains real-time synchronization with the physical asset through continuous data streams from live sensor measurements, ensuring the digital representation reflects the current physical state with minimal latency for critical operational monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system employs a Digital Shadow Reference Model as a UML class diagram to define the structure of contextual data traces, their aggregation, and abstraction for manufacturing applications, orchestrating the integration of physical data into the digital representation through standardized component interfaces and data flow mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol; Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are implemented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for structural modeling across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), with methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component & connector perspectives, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and code in C++/Java for detailed behavior specification. Data management involves handling heterogeneous models across diverse notations and tools to integrate stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical to virtual via MQTT protocol, transmitting time-series sensor data including input current and output angle. Live measurements are streamed continuously for real-time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between physical and virtual components uses MQTT protocol for sensor data transmission; network topology, bandwidth, and redundancy specifications are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows a structured methodology including Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, and model derivation steps as detailed in Section II.C, with Section IV.A demonstrating application in friction model dynamics. Evolution is managed through a dynamic pipeline enabling real-time exchange of friction models, facilitating continuous adaptation of the digital twin to physical system changes via automated data synchronization and model refinement.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides integrated visualization within a unified dashboard to detect friction loss and improve robot motion accuracy through real-time analytics and data-driven insights.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system implements Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized asset management, adheres to Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for requirements and architecture modeling, and employs UML class diagrams for reference model definitions, with architecture description languages (ADLs) such as AADL, ACME, and MontiArc ensuring interoperability and component-based system descriptions.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: Correctly identifies the MABI MAX 100 as a six-axis serial robot for manufacturing and highlights the focus on friction modeling. However, it provides a generic description of components ('base axis, joints, mechanical structure') while the source text lists specific technical details like the 'permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', 'cycloidal gear', and 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller. Supported by quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'enables the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'.

Accurately lists the core services: continuous friction identification, accuracy improvement, and dashboard visualization. However, it includes unsupported speculative details regarding target users ('manufacturing engineers and maintenance personnel') and service intent ('troubleshooting'). It also summarizes the dashboard data generally rather than using the specific metrics (torque, angular velocity) found in the text. Supported by quotes: 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'visualization in a dashboard'.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate summary of the tools and methodologies found in Section II.A of the source text. It correctly lists MBSE languages, specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, etc.), ADLs, and simulation tools. Evidence: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and 'methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT.'

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source text supports the real-time nature of the system. The abstract mentions combining models with 'live data during the operation', and the introduction describes digital shadows as 'real-time, data-driven representations'. Furthermore, Section IV.A confirms that sensor data is 'streamed via MQTT'. The extraction accurately summarizes these elements, describing continuous data streams and real-time synchronization.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is accurate as the text focuses on a single digital shadow for a specific robot axis and does not discuss multiple twin instances or decentralized architectures. Supported by quotes: 'FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow' and 'focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100... first axis'.

The extraction is highly specific and well-supported by Section II.B, which details the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' as a 'UML class diagram' that defines 'contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction'. The extraction correctly captures the architectural approach of using reference models to orchestrate data integration through component interfaces and data flow mechanisms.

The extraction accurately cites the use of the MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming and the implementation of Asset Administration Shells (AAS). This is directly supported by the text in the Abstract ('implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)') and Section IV ('We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT').

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate and detailed, closely mirroring the text in Section II.A regarding MBSE and modeling tools. It correctly lists the abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product), specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), Architecture Description Languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), behavioral modeling methods (MontiArc Automaton, SysML v1.7 state machines), and integration tools (MathWorks Simulink, C++, Java). It also captures the challenge of handling 'models in different modeling notations and different tools' and integrating 'heterogeneous stakeholders'.

The extraction claims 'Not in Document', but Section III explicitly describes the physical acting components of the robot. Specifically, it states: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with the current I_M as input and torque τ_M as output.' It further describes the transmission system: 'a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear'. Since the extraction denies the presence of information that is clearly in the text, it is incorrect.

The extraction claims 'Not in Document', but Section III provides details on the physical sensing infrastructure. The text states: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.' It also mentions that data 'time series of all sensors... are streamed via MQTT. These are the input current and the output angle.' Since the extraction denies the presence of this specific technical information, it is incorrect.

The extraction claims 'Not in Document'. While there is no dedicated section titled 'fidelity', the document discusses validation concepts in Section IV.A. It defines the solution scope as 'the deviation of simulated data versus measured data' and describes the process of comparing 'reference values and simulated values which we compare against the reference values'. It mentions using 'executable simulation models... [to] simulat[e] the expected behavior of the system' and comparing this against sensor data. The text also notes the goal is to 'derive the friction... [to] improve the robot control’s accuracy,' implying a validity/accuracy check. Although not exhaustive, relevant information regarding comparing simulation to measurement (validation) exists, making the 'Not in Document' claim false.

There is no mention of data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance regulations, or consent management within the provided snippets.

The document describes the physical system, mentioning that 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range' as a hardware constraint. However, it does not discuss cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms for the digital shadow or data systems. Consequently, the specific security and safety measures defined in the characteristic description are not present in the text.
